name: 100-gecs
description: This is hyperpop at its most extreme and gleefully overloaded — an internet-fried collision of pop punk, ska, dubstep, nightcore, trance, happy hardcore and emo crammed into one chaotic, hyper-referential blender. Vocals arrive pitched-up and drowned in auto-tune, flipping from candy-sweet chipmunk hooks to bratty, screamed catharsis. Beats are glitched-out and warped, stacked with stadium-sized trap drums, twangy skate-punk guitars, ravey arpeggios and a wall of distorted, blown-out bass mixed to the edge of destruction. Tempos lurch wildly, splicing breakneck nightcore sprints into dubstep wobble drops and ska upstrokes without warning. The result is abrasive yet absurdly catchy, ironic but emotionally raw, a maximalist sugar-rush that treats every genre as a toy and every limiter as a suggestion. It is loud, deliberately tasteless, deeply online, and unmistakably modern — the sound of pop tropes pushed past their breaking point into joyful, addictive overload that rewires what a hook can be.

description: An ambient project that became a catalyst for the dreampunk movement, this music turns the late-night city into an immersive emotional landscape. Built without samples, it leans on slowly evolving synthesizer pads, distant cascading piano, deep drones and washes of cavernous reverb that stretch every sound toward the horizon. Rain and urban field recordings drift through the mix, distant sirens and traffic blurring into the haze until the boundary between music and place dissolves. The tempo barely moves, more tidal than rhythmic, letting each chord bloom and decay across widescreen stereo space. The result is a vague, neon-lit utopia steeped in romantic melancholy and longing, equal parts cyber-future cruise and healing nocturne. Tracks unfold like post-rock structures dissolved into vapor, with synth processing smearing edges into soft focus. It is melancholic, weightless and profoundly atmospheric, music for empty streets glowing under a slow, electric midnight.

name: alcest
description: Alcest is the French project that more or less invented blackgaze — a fusion of the icy tremolo guitars and blast-beats of atmospheric black metal with the warm, hazy melody and reverb-drenched textures of shoegaze. Founded as the vehicle for one musician's vision of a luminous 'other world', it traded black metal's bleakness for something radiant and nostalgic. Cascading layers of distorted guitar shimmer over double-kick drumming, while clean, ethereal tenor vocals float dreamily above — often wordless, occasionally giving way to distant black-metal rasps. The production is vast and washed in reverb, built to evoke memory, nature and half-remembered childhood. Dynamics swing from fragile, almost folk-like passages to towering walls of melodic distortion and back. Drop it into Suno to rebuild that blackgaze signature: tremolo-picked shimmering guitars, blast-beat-to-post-rock dynamics, ethereal clean vocals and a cavernous reverberant mix. Reach for it on atmospheric, melancholic-yet-uplifting tracks, cinematic post-metal, meditation and nature imagery, film and game scoring, or anything that needs beauty and heaviness in the same breath.

name: alexander-vertinsky
description: An intimate, theatrical strain of Russian emigre art-song born in the cabarets and salons of the early twentieth century and carried across Europe, the Orient and beyond. These miniature “ariettes” play less like songs than like staged confessions: each one a small drama with a wistful hero, sketched in the half-light of a candlelit cabaret. The voice is a soft, melodic recitative that hovers between speech and melody, colored by a signature guttural rolled “r” and an exquisitely mannered, aristocratic diction. Phrasing is languid and confiding, dramatized with sighs, pauses and a faintly ironic melancholy. Accompaniment is restrained and chamber-scaled — a tender piano, perhaps a violin, light strings or muted guitar — never crowding the voice. The mood is nostalgic, decadent and tinged with exile: lost loves, faraway harbors, pale dawns and the elegant sorrow of a vanished world. The recording feel is vintage, warm and slightly worn, like a treasured pre-war shellac disc.

name: alfa
description: Forged in Moscow at the dawn of the 1980s, this band built a distinctly Soviet brand of melodic hard rock, where roaring electric guitar shared the spotlight with bright, prominent keyboards. Founded by a keyboard player who had cut his teeth in the country's heavier scene, the group leaned on warm analog synthesizers, organ-like pads and punchy guitar riffs to frame anthemic, sing-along choruses. The vocals are unmistakably male and full-throated, half-crooned and half-belted, carrying a swagger that suited their best-loved tracks, including a rocked-up setting of Sergei Yesenin's poetry. The production carries the slightly raw, magnetic-tape character of early-80s underground tape albums that spread through discos across the USSR. Tuneful, theatrical and faintly melancholic beneath the bravado, the sound mixes hard-rock muscle with pop accessibility, danceable mid-tempo grooves and soaring melodic hooks. It is the sound of late-Soviet youth: defiant, romantic and built for stadium-sized singalongs.
prompt: 1980s Soviet melodic hard rock, anthemic male vocal sung in Russian, half-crooned half-belted with swagger and romantic melancholy.

name: bonobo
description: Behind the name Bonobo is Simon Green, a Brighton-raised producer who first surfaced on Tru Thoughts in 1999 before signing to Coldcut's Ninja Tune in 2001. Early records like Dial 'M' for Monkey leaned trip-hop, but his sound widened steadily, folding jazz, soul and field-recorded textures into patient, sample-built grooves. Black Sands (2010) was the turning point: tracks such as "Eyesdown" paired Andreya Triana's vocals with intricate, hand-stitched arrangements that felt closer to composition than to beat-making. The North Borders (2013) brought in Erykah Badu, and 2017's Migration — written while Green lived out of suitcases on tour, with no fixed home for nearly a year — earned two Grammy nominations, including Best Dance/Electronic Album. What truly sets him apart is the live show: since the Black Sands era he has toured a full ensemble of keys, guitar, saxophone, strings and drums, reworking studio pieces into improvised, breathing performances at Coachella, Glastonbury and countless sold-out halls. name: chet-baker
description: Born in Yale, Oklahoma in 1929, Chet Baker turned up on the Los Angeles scene just as cool jazz was taking shape, and Charlie Parker hand-picked him for a run of West Coast dates in 1952. That same year he joined the pianoless Gerry Mulligan Quartet, where his trumpet and Mulligan's baritone sax traded airy counterpoint instead of doubling melody lines in unison. His hushed, almost weightless reading of "My Funny Valentine" became the piece he was forever said to own. Then he started singing, and that changed everything. The androgynous, fragile croon on Chet Baker Sings (1954) and It Could Happen to You (1958) sounded less like a performance than a confession breathed straight into the microphone, and his James Dean looks turned him into a magazine-cover idol nicknamed the Prince of Cool. Heroin shadowed the rest of his life: jail terms on three continents, a 1966 beating that wrecked his teeth and embouchure and forced years of painful retraining, and a hard-won European comeback in the late 1970s and 80s. He died in Amsterdam in 1988 after a fall from a hotel window, leaving behind one of the most tender, vulnerable sounds jazz has ever produced.

name: gojira
description: It started in a garage in the coastal town of Ondres in 1996, where two teenage brothers, Joe and Mario Duplantier, plugged in under the name Godzilla. Joined by guitarist Christian Andreu and bassist Jean-Michel Labadie, the French quartet became Gojira in 2001 and has kept the exact same lineup ever since — a rarity in metal. Terra Incognita and The Link earned them a fierce European following, but it was 2005's From Mars to Sirius, carried by "Flying Whales" and "Ocean Planet," that pushed them onto the world stage and into lists of the greatest metal albums ever cut. Their signature is precision: surgical death-metal riffing welded to slow-burn groove, vast atmosphere, and those uncanny pinch-harmonic squeals that echo whale song. Lyrics dwell on spirituality, philosophy and the planet's fragile future. The grief-haunted Magma (2016) and the granite-heavy Fortitude (2021) broadened their audience, and in 2024 they made history as the first metal band to perform at an Olympic opening ceremony, soon followed by a Grammy win. The result is music that hits like a tidal wave yet leaves you weirdly elevated — heavy without ever turning murky, every note ringing clear through the wall of distortion.

Where the group's 1990s output was raw, garage-bred horror-punk, this era trades grit for grandeur. Full orchestral arrangements sweep beneath the songs, gothic choirs swell on the choruses, and an operatic sense of scene-setting frames every verse, yet the engine underneath is still pure punk: galloping drums, driving guitars and breathless forward momentum. The defining feature is the macabre dual-vocal storytelling — characters trading lines, snarling and declaiming through grim fairy-tale narratives of murder and revenge. The style prompt captures this bombastic, cinematic register: orchestral horror-punk, gothic choirs, theatrical narrative vocals and relentless drive. Use it for dark musical-theatre numbers, villain anthems, macabre story-songs and any track that wants both punk adrenaline and operatic spectacle. For the raw early sound, see the band's separate classic-era entry.
prompt: symphonic horror-punk rock opera, theatrical macabre storytelling, lush orchestral arrangements with strings and brass, driving distorted power chords,

They envisioned a world where man and machine coexisted in seamless harmony, and their music became the soundtrack to that vision, defined by precise vocoders, crisp drum machines, and an undeniable sense of purpose.

name: ladysmith-black-mambazo
description: Ladysmith Black Mambazo are South Africa's foremost masters of isicathamiya — the gentle Zulu a cappella tradition born in migrant-worker hostels, built on tight, swaying close harmonies led by a deep, resonant bass voice. They rose to global fame in the 1980s as the warm vocal sound that carried Zulu music across the world, and have remained its best-known ambassadors ever since. There are no instruments at all: the music is woven purely from human voices — a sonorous bass anchor calling phrases, with the group answering in lush, interlocking harmony and soft, breath-light rhythmic articulation that mimics careful, tiptoeing footwork. The mood is peaceful, uplifting and deeply communal, equal parts hymn, lullaby and joyful celebration. The style prompt captures it: rich Zulu close-harmony group vocals, a deep bass lead, hushed rhythmic call-and-response and a serene, sunlit warmth. Use it for uplifting a cappella pieces, spiritual and ceremonial moments, gentle communal anthems, or any track that wants pure unaccompanied human harmony and quiet joy.

Lyrically and tonally it evokes sunbleached California highways, doomed romance, old-money decadence and a longing for a glamorous past that may never have existed. Tempos sit slow and hypnotic, letting each phrase bleed into the next with languid, narcotic ease. There is a deliberate tension between modern hip-hop-inflected rhythm and timeless 1950s-60s pop orchestration, giving the music a dreamlike, out-of-time quality. The style prompt bottles that haze: deep dropping bass, swimming tape delays, ghostly vocal fragments and warm, saturated low-fi murk where space matters as much as sound. name: m83
description: A French electronic project built on maximalism: layers upon layers of sound designed to overwhelm and surround the listener. The signature is enormous reverb-drenched synth walls — fat analog leads, sawtooth pads and 80s-tinged arpeggios that swell into stadium-sized, anthemic builds. Drum machines snap underneath sparse, dynamic verses before bursting into dense, euphoric choruses where sustained synths fill every channel. Vocals are soft and ethereal, multilayered and panned wide, set adrift on beds of reverb and delay rather than pushed front and center. The overall mood is nostalgic and cinematic, evoking endless desert skies, neon dusk and the bittersweet ache of youth. Influences from John Hughes-era teen films and shoegaze texture meet widescreen electronic production, producing music that feels both intimate and vast. Track structures move from hushed restraint to explosive, tear-jerking release, designed for headphones, road trips and emotional crescendos.

Vocals are warm, mellow and conversational — a relaxed male croon that slips between deadpan charm and quiet melancholy, layered into soft harmonies that feel half-asleep. Recorded with a homemade, four-track warmth, the production trades polish for intimacy and a nostalgic glow. name: meshuggah
description: Emerging from Sweden in the late 1980s and reaching full force across the 1990s and 2000s, this band became the architect of djent and a cornerstone of modern extreme progressive metal. Landmark works like "Destroy Erase Improve," "Catch Thirtythree" and "obZen" define the sound: hyper-precise palm-muted riffs played on radically down-tuned eight-string guitars, arranged in shifting polymetric and polyrhythmic patterns that float over an unwavering 4/4 pulse. The effect is mechanical, robotic, almost mathematical, yet crushing and hypnotic. name: paco-de-lucia

The music breathes through traditional Andalusian forms — the driving twelve-beat compás of bulerías, the festive sway of rumba, the gravity of soleá — propelled by syncopated palmas handclaps, foot-stomping zapateado, and the warm slap of the cajón. Later work folds in jazz harmony, double bass, and flute, opening flamenco toward fusion while keeping its raw duende intact. name: ravi-shankar
description: This is the sound of Hindustani classical music at its most expressive: the sitar elevated from a regional instrument to a vehicle of profound spiritual and technical mastery. Each performance unfolds as a raga, a melodic framework woven from microtonal bends called meend, shimmering sympathetic strings, and cascading ornaments that seem to bend time itself. The journey begins with a slow, unmetered alap, an introspective meditation that gradually introduces the raga's character note by note. As the tabla enters, intricate rhythmic cycles known as tala lock into dialogue with the melody, building tension through call-and-response. The pace quickens through jor and finally erupts into the dazzling jhala, a virtuosic flurry of rapid plucking against the steady drone of the tanpura. Largely instrumental and deeply meditative, the music balances disciplined structure with improvisational freedom, carrying centuries of North Indian tradition while opening it to listeners around the world. It is contemplative, hypnotic, and breathtakingly virtuosic.

Their sound is an intoxicating, often jarring, blend of punk rock aggression, traditional Russian folk melodies, and a darkly theatrical, almost operatic, narrative style. With frontman Yuri 'Khot' Klinskikh’s distinctive, gravelly voice leading the charge, Sektor Gaza captured the disillusionment, black humor, and everyday struggles of a nation in flux, making them an essential, if sometimes controversial, voice of their era.

name: selda-bagcan
description: Selda Bağcan is the fierce, unmistakable voice of Turkish Anatolian rock, a folk singer who took the long-necked saz, ran it through fuzz and wah pedals, and fused ancient Anatolian melody with the heat of late-1970s psychedelia and political protest, defiance that saw her jailed for her songs. Her records pair raw, hypnotic folk modes with a funky rhythm section: a driving electric saz buzzing with distortion, snaking wah guitar, deep grooving bass and earthy hand-played percussion that locks into an irresistible, head-nodding pulse. Over it all rides her voice, soulful, impassioned and powerful, carrying centuries of melody with modern grit. Endlessly sampled today, the sound is groovy, earthy and instantly recognizable. The style prompt captures it: fuzzed electric saz, funky wah guitar, hypnotic modal folk and a defiant, heartfelt lead. Use it for psychedelic vintage grooves, gritty cinematic montages, protest and rebellion themes, or any track wanting raw 1970s soul with an Eastern modal edge.
prompt: 1970s Anatolian psych-folk rock, fuzzed-out electric saz and bağlama, hypnotic Turkish folk modes, funky wah-wah guitar, driving combo

The production is glossy millennial synth-pop — bright keyboard layers, electronic dance beats, programmed grooves and arrangements threaded with distinctly Turkish melodic motifs and ornamental runs. Choruses arrive relentlessly catchy, engineered to lodge in memory on first listen, with that East-meets-club tension that defined an era of Turkish radio. The ready-made Suno style prompt captures energetic 2000s Turkish dance-pop: bright synth-pop arrangement, electronic beat, Turkish melodic sensibility and an irresistible hook-driven chorus. name: sergey-nikitin
description: Sergey Nikitin occupies a treasured corner of Russian bard music (авторская песня / KSP) — a singer-composer who set the poetry of the Soviet intelligentsia to gentle, warm acoustic melodies from the 1970s onward. Often performing in duet with his wife Tatyana, his intimate fingerpicked guitar arrangements and soft conversational tenor became the soundtrack of kitchen gatherings, campfire singalongs and beloved Soviet films (his "Александра" from "Москва слезам не верит", "Под музыку Вивальди", "Брич-Мулла"). The bard aesthetic centers on poetic literary lyrics, unforced sincere vocal delivery, intricate classical-influenced acoustic guitar, and chamber intimacy — no drums, no production gloss, just voice and guitar. For producers chasing warm Soviet bard intimacy, intellectual-romantic acoustic balladry, or tender fingerpicked guitar-and-voice chamber song, Nikitin is the defining reference.

name: sigur-ros
description: Sigur Ros are Iceland's masters of glacial post-rock, makers of music as vast and weightless as their frozen homeland. At the centre is Jonsi's fragile falsetto, often sung in an invented language so the voice becomes pure texture and feeling rather than meaning. It floats over bowed-guitar swells, a guitar played with a cello bow to produce long, keening tones, layered with ambient shimmer, glockenspiel, piano and slowly blooming orchestral strings. Their songs are studies in patience: they begin in near-silence, a whisper of a melody, then build across minutes from a hush to a towering, euphoric roar before dissolving again. The mood is at once melancholic and transcendent, weeping and elated. The Suno style-prompt captures that arc: ethereal wordless vocals, ambient shimmer, bowed-guitar swells and patient builds to a glacial, soaring climax. Use it for emotional film peaks, nature and landscape sequences, introspective or grieving moments, or any track that must rise slowly from intimacy to overwhelming release.
